text: Explore the clinical relevance of genes & variants ClinGen is a National Institutes of Health (NIH)-funded resource dedicated to building a central resource that defines the clinical relevance of genes and variants for use in precision medicine and research.

ClinGen is defining the clinical relevance of genes and variants Founded in 2013 by the National Human Genome Research Institute, ClinGen is a collaborative effort, involving over 2,800 contributors from 77 countries.

Advancing genomic knowledge through global curation In Genetics in Medicine, a special article from ClinGen describing the methods of genomic curation and development of software and infrastructure needed to support a global consortium capable of large-scale evidence-based curation.

A list of recommendations released by ClinGen or endorsed by ClinGen for using the ACMG/AMP Variant Interpretation Guidelines criteria. View ClinGen's list of clinical laboratories meeting minimum requirements for data sharing to support quality assurance. Visit our Community Curation page to learn more about volunteer biocuration opportunities.

Take a brief survey to tell us about your interests and desired level of involvement. We will pair you with an appropriate curation activity and/or Expert Panel. New to ClinGen? Learn how to get started. Learn more about our efforts by choosing one of the options below.

Can variation in this gene cause disease? Are there actions that could be taken to improve outcomes for patients with this genetic risk? Does loss or gain of a copy of this gene or genomic region result in disease? Somatic Cancer Clinical Domain Working Group curates the clinical significance of genomic anomalies associated with different cancer types within the following diseases specific task forces - pediatric cancers, pancreatic cancer, lung cancer and genitourinary cancers Baseline annotation focuses on annotating evidence in the biomedical literature in a structured and standardized way, thus supporting our expert panels, working groups, and curation processes.

Evidence-based classification of genes implicated in craniosynostosis disorders using the ClinGen curation framework This sub-survey is assigned to participants who indicated genetic testing was complete during a past or current pregnancy. The survey intends to collect additional information about the pregnancy's diagnosis, treatments, and care. The linked survey is in English.

Sequence Data Sharing Report Update Preferences ClinGen API platform for classification of human genetic variants ClinGen Quarterly Update January - March 2026 ClinGen Gene Curation Standardized Evidence Summary Text Review the GenomeConnect consent form. If you are interested in participating in GenomeConnect, this consent form can be completed online at genomeconnect. org.
